reference: GATE CS — Operating System / Processes and Threads

map.condition: Find max processes that avoid deadlock with shared resources
Check if a safe sequence exists (Banker's Algorithm)
Detect deadlock from RAG with single-instance resources
Detect deadlock from RAG with multi-instance resources
Deadlock-free guarantee with n processes, R resources, max k each

formulas.name: Mutual Exclusion Condition
formulas.latex: \text{At least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode}
formulas.plain: At least one resource must be held in a non-sharable (exclusive) mode.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying whether mutual exclusion condition for deadlock holds.
formulas.trap: Mutual exclusion alone does not cause deadlock; all four conditions must hold simultaneously.

formulas.name: Hold and Wait Condition
formulas.latex: \text{A process holding } \ge 1 \text{ resource is waiting to acquire more resources}
formulas.plain: A process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying the hold-and-wait necessary condition for deadlock.
formulas.trap: Prevention requires either requesting all resources at once (low utilization) or releasing before requesting new ones.

formulas.name: No Preemption Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process holding them}
formulas.plain: Resources cannot be preempted; a resource can be released only voluntarily by the process holding it.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying the no-preemption necessary condition for deadlock.
formulas.trap: Allowing preemption prevents this condition but may cause starvation.

formulas.name: Circular Wait Condition
formulas.latex: \exists \{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_n\} : P_0 \to P_1 \to \cdots \to P_n \to P_0
formulas.plain: There exists a circular chain: P0 waits for P1, P1 waits for P2, ..., Pn waits for P0.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying the circular-wait necessary condition; imposed ordering of resources prevents this.

terms.means: Pi is waiting for a resource held by Pj

formulas.trap: Circular wait is sufficient to detect deadlock only when resources have single instances.

formulas.name: Maximum Processes for Deadlock-Free System
formulas.plain: n <= (R - 1) / (N_max - 1) + 1, where n = number of processes, R = total resources, N_max = max need per process.
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the largest number of processes guaranteed deadlock-free with R identical resources when each process needs at most N_max.

terms.means: number of concurrent processes
total number of resource instances
maximum resource need of each process

formulas.trap: Condition n*(N_max-1)+1 <= R guarantees deadlock-free. Deadlock is possible when n*(N_max-1) >= R.

formulas.name: Deadlock-Free Sufficient Condition
formulas.plain: n*(k-1)+1 <= R implies the system is guaranteed to be deadlock-free.
formulas.whenToUse: Quick check: given n processes each needing at most k resources and R total, is the system safe?

terms.means: number of processes
maximum resource demand per process
total resource instances available

formulas.trap: This gives a sufficient (not necessary) condition; the system may be safe even when violated, but Banker's must be run to confirm.

formulas.name: Need Matrix
formulas.whenToUse: Computing remaining resource need for each process in Banker's algorithm.

terms.means: maximum demand of process i for resource j
currently allocated units of resource j to process i
remaining need of process i for resource j

formulas.trap: If Need[i] <= Available, process i can potentially run to completion and release its resources.

formulas.name: Available Update on Process Completion
formulas.plain: Available += Allocation[i] when process Pi finishes (releases all resources).
formulas.whenToUse: Simulating Banker's algorithm: once a process completes, add its allocations back to Available.

terms.means: vector of currently available resource instances
resource vector currently held by process i

formulas.trap: Processes must be simulated in a valid safe sequence order; any order that allows all to finish is a safe sequence.

formulas.name: Safe State Definition
formulas.latex: \text{Safe State} \iff \exists \text{ safe sequence } \langle P_{\sigma(1)}, P_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, P_{\sigma(n)} \rangle
formulas.plain: A state is safe if there exists at least one safe sequence in which all processes can complete.
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding if a resource-allocation state is safe before granting a request.

terms.means: permutation of process indices forming a safe sequence

formulas.trap: Safe state => no deadlock; unsafe state does NOT guarantee deadlock, but deadlock is possible.

formulas.name: Request Grant Condition (Banker's)
formulas.plain: Grant request Ri iff Ri <= Need[i] AND Ri <= Available, and the resulting state is safe.
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding whether to immediately grant or queue a resource request in Banker's algorithm.

terms.means: resource request vector from process i

formulas.trap: Even if both conditions hold, pretend to allocate and run the safety algorithm; grant only if result is safe.

formulas.name: Deadlock in Single-Instance RAG
formulas.plain: For single-instance resources: deadlock exists iff there is a cycle in the Resource Allocation Graph.
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting deadlock using RAG when each resource type has exactly one instance.

terms.means: Resource Allocation Graph: edges from resource to process (assignment) and process to resource (request)

formulas.trap: A cycle in RAG is necessary AND sufficient for deadlock only when all resources have single instances.

formulas.name: Deadlock in Multi-Instance RAG
formulas.plain: For multi-instance resources: a cycle in RAG is necessary but NOT sufficient; a knot (every node reachable from it is also in the cycle) is needed.
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing RAG when resource types have multiple instances.

terms.means: a set of nodes where every node can reach every other node in the set and no node can escape the set

formulas.trap: A cycle without a knot does NOT necessarily mean deadlock in multi-instance RAG — use Banker's or detection algorithm instead.

formulas.name: RAG Edge Types
formulas.latex: P_i \to R_j : \text{request edge}; \quad R_j \to P_i : \text{assignment edge}
formulas.plain: Pi -> Rj: process Pi is requesting resource Rj. Rj -> Pi: resource Rj is assigned to process Pi.
formulas.whenToUse: Reading and constructing a Resource Allocation Graph.

terms.means: process node
resource type node (with dots for instances)

formulas.trap: In a claim edge variant (for avoidance), a dashed edge Pi -> Rj means Pi may request Rj in the future but hasn't yet.

formulas.name: Deadlock Detection Algorithm (Multi-Instance)
formulas.plain: Initialize Work = Available. Finish[i] = true if Allocation[i] is all zeros. Find i where Finish[i]=false and Request[i]<=Work; set Work+=Allocation[i], Finish[i]=true. Repeat. Deadlock if any Finish[i]=false.
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting deadlock in a system that allows deadlock to occur (no prevention/avoidance).

terms.means: available resources during detection simulation
true if process i can complete
current outstanding request of process i

formulas.trap: Unlike Banker's, Request[i] is actual current request, not maximum remaining need.

formulas.name: Wait-For Graph (Single-Instance Detection)
formulas.latex: P_i \to P_j \in \text{WFG} \iff P_i \text{ is waiting for a resource held by } P_j
formulas.plain: In a Wait-For Graph, edge Pi->Pj means Pi is waiting for Pi to release a resource. Deadlock iff cycle in WFG.
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting deadlock in single-instance resource systems by simplifying the RAG.

terms.means: Wait-For Graph derived from RAG by removing resource nodes

formulas.trap: WFG is derived from RAG by removing resource nodes; only applicable to single-instance resources.

formulas.name: Wait-Die Scheme (Non-Preemptive)
formulas.latex: \text{If } T_i < T_j: P_i \text{ waits}; \quad \text{If } T_i > T_j: P_i \text{ dies (restarts)}
formulas.plain: Wait-Die: older process (smaller timestamp) waits for younger; younger process dies and restarts. Non-preemptive.
formulas.whenToUse: Deadlock prevention using timestamps; process is not preempted, but younger requests are aborted.

terms.means: timestamp (age) of process Pi; smaller = older

formulas.trap: In Wait-Die, ONLY older waits for younger. Dying process restarts with original timestamp (not new).

formulas.name: Wound-Wait Scheme (Preemptive)
formulas.latex: \text{If } T_i < T_j: P_j \text{ is wounded (preempted/dies)}; \quad \text{If } T_i > T_j: P_i \text{ waits}
formulas.plain: Wound-Wait: older process wounds (preempts) younger; younger waits for older. Preemptive.
formulas.whenToUse: Deadlock prevention using timestamps; older processes can preempt younger ones.

terms.means: timestamp of process Pi; smaller = older

formulas.trap: Wound-Wait is opposite of Wait-Die: older WOUNDS younger (not waits); younger WAITS for older (not dies).

formulas.name: Resource Ordering (Circular-Wait Prevention)
formulas.latex: \forall i < j: \text{process must request } R_i \text{ before } R_j \implies \text{no circular wait}
formulas.plain: Assign a total ordering to resources; processes must request resources only in increasing order. Eliminates circular wait.
formulas.whenToUse: Preventing deadlock by eliminating the circular-wait condition through resource numbering.
formulas.trap: Resource ordering eliminates circular wait but reduces flexibility and can cause unnecessary waiting.
